title: ‘Project Hail Mary’ Will Amaze! Amaze! Amaze! With Phil Lord and Chris Miller
format: Sean and Amanda open on the low Oscar ratings and a run of news, review Lord & Miller's 'Project Hail Mary,' then Sean talks to the directors about building the thing by hand.

segments.heading: The news
segments.note: Oscar ratings, a Spider-Man trailer, and Netflix's theatrical flirtation

films.title: Spider-Man: Brand New Day
films.note: The new trailer, which Amanda watched during a manicure. Destin Daniel Cretton takes over from Jon Watts; Jon Bernthal's Punisher, Michael Mando's Scorpion, a returning Hulk, and a hidden Sadie Sink (Sean's guess: Jean Grey). Both thought it looked small and un-special.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The nearly-$2B comparison point; the mind-wipe that this movie is picking up from.

films.title: Solo: A Star Wars Story
films.note: Sean rewatched it with his daughter and calls it the first not-special Star Wars movie. Later tied back to Lord & Miller, who shot chunks of it before Lucasfilm replaced them with Ron Howard.

films.title: A Minecraft Movie
films.note: Context for Kirsten Dunst joining the sequel, which she publicly campaigned for, in her words, to make 'a pile of cash.'

films.title: Mercy
films.note: Cited as a check-cashing role: Rebecca Ferguson playing an AI, in the running-bit about 'would you make money off an AI friend named Money.'

films.title: Her
films.note: Reference point for the 'comedian dating an AI chatbot and bringing her places' story.

films.title: The Shrouds
films.note: Also invoked in the AI-friend bit: Vincent Cassel and the chatbot who's secretly Guy Pearce.

films.title: The Adventures of Cliff Booth
films.note: The Netflix / Tarantino-written, Fincher-directed feature whose August date was scrubbed from a Deadline report. Sean speculates Sony could put it in theaters with Netflix.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The prior Cliff Booth movie, a Sony-distributed summer release: Sean's evidence the sequel plays as summer.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: Apple's model of pairing with a legacy studio (Paramount) for a theatrical release; source of the two-year single-still rollout Sean expects for Scorsese's next.

films.title: F1
films.note: Apple's last-summer theatrical, distributed with Warner Bros.

films.title: K-Pop Demon Hunters
films.note: The Sony Pictures Animation hit, cited to argue Netflix and Sony already have a like-meets-like relationship.

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: One of the Netflix films they keep saying should get a real theatrical run.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: The other Netflix property they think is 'bizarre' not to open in theaters at scale.

films.title: Wuthering Heights
films.note: Emerald Fennell passed on Netflix to make it at Warner Bros, evidence filmmakers want theatrical.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Ryan Coogler's Warner Bros. deal (print control, a real theatrical commitment) as the counter-model; also a lingering Oscar competitor that had been out almost a year by the ceremony.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Dropped as a byword for dreaming big, apropos of Sean's Oscars-attendance fantasies.

films.title: Crime 101
films.note: First surfaces here (the Loews helicopter pad) before Chris Miller names it his last-great-thing in the interview.

films.title: Project Hail Mary
films.note: Lord & Miller's first directing job in a decade, from Drew Goddard's script of Andy Weir's novel (the same Weir/Goddard pairing as 'The Martian'). Ryan Gosling (who produced) as Ryland Grace, a middle-school science teacher with a PhD, waking with amnesia on a last-chance mission against Astrophage dimming the sun; Sandra Hüller as Ava Stratton. Both loved it, the first 90 minutes 'truly magical,' and Amanda floats it as better than 'The Martian.' The whole thing feels built by hand: sets on stilts, a real puppet Rocky (Neal Scanlan's creature, James Ortiz and the 'Rocketeers' puppeteering), Greg Fraser's imperfectionist camera, Daniel Pemberton's percussive-then-choral score. Amanda's alien since E.T. The knock: it's too long and ends five different times, a baggy last 15 to 25 minutes.

segments.heading: Phil Lord and Chris Miller on ‘Project Hail Mary’
segments.guest: Phil Lord and Chris Miller on

films.title: Project Hail Mary
films.note: Ryan Gosling and Amy Pascal sent them Weir's manuscript pre-publication; producer Adithya Sood (who'd found 'The Martian' as an e-book) told them it was their next movie. On making Rocky a real five-puppeteer creature so Gosling had a scene partner, building the ship twice (vertical then horizontal for the spin-gravity flip), a green-screen-free shoot, the 'Greg vibes pass,' the infrared-Astrophage chicken-wire rig, and 11 test screenings. Two Oscar-winning HODs (Fraser, Paul Lambert) called it the most complex film they'd made: 'and we made two Dunes.'

films.title: Crime 101
films.note: Chris Miller's last-great-thing: Bart Layton's heist picture, seen at the CityWalk IMAX. 'LA for LA,' one-last-job crime cinema that turns the 101 freeway into poetry.

films.title: Papillon
films.note: Phil Lord's last-great-thing: an Oscar-nominated animated short, a painting-and-repainting story about a swimmer, which he watched with his kids and praised on technique.
